skill.name: Azure.ResourceManager.Fabric (.NET)
skill.description: Azure Resource Manager SDK for Fabric in .NET. Use for management plane operations: provisioning, scaling, suspending/resuming Microsoft Fabric capacities, checking name availability, and listing SKUs via Azure Resource Manager.
skill.summary: Management plane SDK for provisioning and managing Microsoft Fabric capacity resources via Azure Resource Manager.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill containing markdown guidance for Azure.ResourceManager.Fabric NuGet package. No executable code, scripts, or network calls. Static analysis correctly identified zero risk (score 0/100). The skill provides legitimate Azure SDK documentation for managing Microsoft Fabric capacities via Azure Resource Manager.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Fabric capacities with .NET SDK
content.value_statement: Developers need to provision and manage Microsoft Fabric compute resources programmatically. This skill provides complete guidance for using Azure.ResourceManager.Fabric to create, scale, suspend, resume, and delete Fabric capacities via Azure Resource Manager.
actual_capabilities: Create and provision Microsoft Fabric capacity resources in Azure
Scale Fabric capacities by updating SKU tiers (F2 through F2048)
Suspend and resume capacities to optimize compute billing
Check name availability for new Fabric capacity resources
List available SKUs and manage capacity administrators
Handle Azure Resource Manager operations with proper authentication
limitations: Management plane only - does not cover data plane operations like workspace or lakehouse management
Requires Azure subscription with appropriate permissions for Fabric resource operations
Capacity provisioning operations can take 5-15 minutes to complete
Fabric capacity names must be globally unique across all Azure subscriptions

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer automating Fabric infrastructure
use_cases.description: Provision and manage Fabric capacities as part of CI/CD pipelines using Azure Resource Manager templates and .NET tooling.

use_cases.title: Cloud Architect designing Fabric governance
use_cases.description: Implement capacity lifecycle management with proper SKU scaling, suspension policies, and administrative access controls.

use_cases.title: Developer building Fabric management tools
use_cases.description: Create custom applications or automation scripts that manage Fabric compute resources programmatically.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Fabric capacity
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Microsoft Fabric capacity in Azure using the Azure.ResourceManager.Fabric SDK. I need an F64 SKU in West US2 with my team member as admin.

prompt_templates.title: Scale Fabric capacity SKU
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to scale an existing Fabric capacity from F32 to F128 SKU using the .NET SDK. Include error handling for provisioning states.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate - SKU scaling with error handling

prompt_templates.title: Suspend and resume capacity for cost optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to suspend my Fabric capacity during off-hours to reduce costs. Write code that suspends the capacity and show me how to resume it when needed.

prompt_templates.title: Complete capacity lifecycle management
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comprehensive example that demonstrates the full lifecycle: check name availability, create capacity with F8 SKU, monitor provisioning state, scale to F64, suspend when idle, and properly delete when no longer needed. Include proper authentication with DefaultAzureCredential and handle all common ARM exceptions.

output_examples.input: Create a Fabric capacity with F64 SKU
output_examples.output: Successfully created capacity 'my-fabric-capacity' in West US2. State: Active. Provisioning: Succeeded. SKU: F64 (Fabric tier). Administrators: admin@contoso.com

output_examples.input: Scale capacity from F64 to F128
output_examples.output: Capacity 'my-fabric-capacity' scaled from F64 to F128. Operation completed in 3 minutes. New state: Active. Billing tier updated.
Capacity 'my-fabric-capacity' suspended successfully. Compute billing stopped. Resource retained. Use ResumeAsync to reactivate.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for authentication to support multiple identity types without hardcoded credentials
Use WaitUntil.Completed for operations that must finish before proceeding, or WaitUntil.Started for parallel execution
Suspend capacities during off-hours to optimize compute billing while retaining the resource
anti_patterns: Never hardcode Azure credentials or connection strings in source code
Do not skip checking provisioning state before performing operations on a capacity
Avoid using CreateAsync instead of CreateOrUpdateAsync when idempotent operations are needed

faq.question: What is the difference between management plane and data plane for Fabric?
faq.answer: Management plane (this SDK) handles capacity provisioning and infrastructure. Data plane handles workspace operations, data items, and analytics. Use this SDK for capacity management, and Microsoft.Fabric.Api for workspace/data operations.

faq.question: How long does it take to provision a Fabric capacity?
faq.answer: Capacity creation typically takes 5-15 minutes. Use WaitUntil.Completed to wait for provisioning to finish, or poll the provisioning state manually.

faq.question: Can I change the SKU of an existing capacity?
faq.answer: Yes, use the UpdateAsync method with a new FabricSku in the patch. Note that SKU changes may cause brief downtime during the scaling operation.

faq.question: What happens when I suspend a capacity?
faq.answer: Suspended capacities stop billing for compute but retain the resource configuration. You can resume at any time. The capacity resource still exists and occupies your subscription quota.

faq.question: Are Fabric capacity names unique per subscription or globally?
faq.answer: Capacity names must be globally unique across all Azure subscriptions. Use CheckNameAvailabilityAsync before creating to verify name availability.

faq.question: Which authentication methods work with DefaultAzureCredential?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential supports Azure CLI login, managed identity, Visual Studio credentials, environment variables, and Azure PowerShell. It tries each method in order until successful.
